ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Veg Kofta or equivalent fried vegetable dumpling dish as of 2024.

allergens: gluten (gram flour / besan may contain traces; check label)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Veg Kofta as a starter consists of 3–4 pieces weighing approximately 200 g, often accompanied by mint chutney or tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Vegetable Cutlet / Fried Vegetable Patty (closest analogue); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central mixed vegetable fritter data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Veg Kofta entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried vegetable cutlet / patty data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central mixed vegetable fritter entries. Input values (energy 225 kcal, protein 5 g, fat 17.5 g, carbohydrate 12.5 g, sodium 225 mg, fibre 3.0 g) are consistent with this analogue range and are retained. Micronutrient values (calcium 25 mg, iron 1.0 mg, vitamin C 10 mg, cholesterol 0 mg, saturated fat 4.0 g, trans fat 0.25 g) are also consistent with the analogue range. Potassium estimated at ~280 mg/100g based on mixed vegetable composition per USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented with University of Sydney GI Database entry for chickpea/gram flour-based fried snack
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Kofta. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogous gram flour (besan) fried snack entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Gram flour has a GI of approximately 39–55 depending on preparation; deep-frying with mixed vegetables raises effective GI slightly. Mixed vegetable and potato content moderately elevates GI above pure besan. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (25 g from 200 g portion) / 100 = 13.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 225 mg/100g (450 mg per 200 g serving) is borderline; deep-frying adds caloric and fat load that may elevate blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g, request pan-fried or baked preparation to reduce fat load, and ask for reduced salt in seasoning, as the 450 mg sodium per standard serving represents approximately 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 52 (low) and dietary fibre at 3.0 g/100g moderate glucose absorption, but high fat at 17.5 g/100g from deep-frying and potato content elevate postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Veg Kofta in moderated portions (100–150 g), preferring pan-fried or baked versions to reduce fat-driven postprandial glucose elevation, and should request reduced potato content to lower the effective glycaemic load of the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat at 17.5 g/100g and saturated fat at 4.0 g/100g from deep-frying, plus trans fat at 0.25 g/100g, present cardiovascular risk at standard serving sizes
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Veg Kofta to occasional consumption, request pan-fried or baked preparation to reduce total and saturated fat, and avoid versions prepared with partially hydrogenated oils to minimise trans fat intake, as the 0.5 g trans fat per 200 g serving contributes meaningfully to cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; nightshade ingredients (capsicum, potatoes, chilli) may trigger inflammatory responses in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried Veg Kofta and request baked or pan-fried versions with nightshade-free vegetable substitutions; turmeric and ginger in the spice blend provide beneficial anti-inflammatory curcumin and gingerol compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Kofta is a popular Indian vegetarian snack and starter dish consisting of spiced mixed vegetable balls or dumplings, typically made from a blend of finely grated or mashed carrots, green beans, cabbage, green peas, capsicum, and potatoes, bound with gram flour (besan) and seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger-garlic paste, and chilli powder. The mixture is shaped into small balls or oval patties and deep-fried or pan-fried until golden and crisp on the outside with a soft, flavourful interior. Veg Kofta is widely served across Indian restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a starter or snack, often accompanied by mint chutney, tamarind sauce, or yogurt dip. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 225 kcal, 5 g protein, 17.5 g fat (4.0 g saturated), 12.5 g carbohydrate, 3.0 g dietary fibre, and 225 mg sodium. The gram flour base gives a lower glycaemic index (estimated GI ~52) compared to wheat-based snacks, and the mixed vegetable composition contributes dietary fibre, vitamin C (10 mg/100g), iron (1.0 mg/100g), and potassium (~280 mg/100g). Clinically, the high fat content from deep-frying is the primary concern for cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and Type 2 Diabetes management; pan-fried or baked versions significantly improve the nutritional profile. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and halal. Nightshade ingredients (capsicum, potatoes) and deep-frying-generated AGEs make this dish unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients in its standard deep-fried form.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Veg Kofta?
Is Veg Kofta good for diabetics?
What is Veg Kofta made of?
Can I eat Veg Kofta if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of Veg Kofta?

confidence_reason: No exact Veg Kofta entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved-source analogues (ICMR-NIN fried vegetable cutlet and USDA FoodData Central mixed vegetable fritter). Input values from the source record are consistent with this analogue range and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from gram flour-based fried snack entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for fried mixed vegetable kofta is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available in the approved sources for this specific fried vegetable composite dish preparation as of 2024. 3. Cultural metadata correction: the input record incorrectly assigned 'Turkish' as primary cuisine with ethnic tags 'Turkish, Kebab, Shawarma' and listed country_of_origin as Singapore. Veg Kofta is an Indian dish with South Asian origins; cuisine has been corrected to 'Indian' and region_of_origin to 'India / South Asia'. The dish is widely served in Singapore but is not of Singaporean origin. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Veg Kofta is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central plant-based fried snack entries. 5. Potassium estimated at ~280 mg/100g based on mixed vegetable composition per USDA FoodData Central; this is an estimate and should be verified against laboratory analysis. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.25 g/100g is retained from input; this is plausible for deep-fried preparations using commercial cooking oils and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central fried snack data. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Veg Kofta has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 8. Multilingual names provided for Indian-origin dish served in Singapore context: Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are included to support Singapore multilingual retrieval. 9. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not correspond to the official HPB nutrient database; this source has been replaced with ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central as approved primary sources.
